155.play: it upon my death, I gave him this wound in the

156.play: THIGH: if the man were alive and would deny it,

157.play: 'zounds, I would make him eat a piece of my sword.

158.play: LANCASTER: This is the strangest tale that ever I heard.

159.play: PRINCE HENRY: This is the strangest fellow, brother John.

160.play: Come, bring your luggage nobly on your back:

161.play: For my part, if a lie may do thee grace,

162.play: I'll gild it with the happiest terms I have.

163.play: The trumpet sounds retreat; the day is ours.

164.play: Come, brother, let us to the highest of the field,

165.play: To see what friends are living, who are dead.

166.play: FALSTAFF: I'll follow, as they say, for reward. He that

167.play: rewards me, God reward him! If I do grow great,

168.play: I'll grow less; for I'll purge, and leave sack, and

169.play: live cleanly as a nobleman should do.

1.play: KING HENRY IV: Thus ever did rebellion find rebuke.

2.play: Ill-spirited Worcester! did not we send grace,

3.play: Pardon and terms of love to all of you?

4.play: And wouldst thou turn our offers contrary?

5.play: Misuse the tenor of thy kinsman's trust?

6.play: Three knights upon our party slain to-day,

7.play: A noble earl and many a creature else

9.play: If like a Christian thou hadst truly borne

11.play: EARL OF WORCESTER: What I have done my safety urged me to;

12.play: And I embrace this fortune patiently,

13.play: Since not to be avoided it falls on me.

14.play: KING HENRY IV: Bear Worcester to the death and Vernon too:

15.play: Other offenders we will pause upon.

17.play: PRINCE HENRY: The noble Scot, Lord Douglas, when he saw

18.play: The fortune of the day quite turn'd from him,

19.play: The noble Percy slain, and all his men

20.play: Upon the foot of fear, fled with the rest;

21.play: And falling from a hill, he was so bruised

22.play: That the pursuers took him. At my tent

23.play: The Douglas is; and I beseech your grace

25.play: KING HENRY IV: With all my heart.

26.play: PRINCE HENRY: Then, brother John of Lancaster, to you

28.play: Go to the Douglas, and deliver him

29.play: Up to his pleasure, ransomless and free:

30.play: His valour shown upon our crests to-day

31.play: Hath taught us how to cherish such high deeds

32.play: Even in the bosom of our adversaries.

33.play: LANCASTER: I thank your grace for this high courtesy,

34.play: Which I shall give away immediately.
